topic.title: Bayes' Rule
topic.question: How does Bayes' rule update beliefs given evidence, and why is the posterior proportional to likelihood times prior?

promptVariant.name: Whiteboard prompt v2 - payoff numbers + sync enforcement

lesson.title: Bayes' Rule: Updating Beliefs with Evidence
lesson.payoff: A medical test that is 99% accurate sounds nearly perfect -- yet a positive result for a disease affecting 1 in 1000 people means less than 9% chance you actually have it. Bayes' rule is the single formula that explains this surprising gap between test accuracy and true probability.
staircase: joint probability as the product of marginal and conditional
conditional probability P(A|B) defined as the fraction of B-worlds in which A also occurs
the symmetry that lets us write the joint two ways and equate them
Bayes' formula derived from that symmetry
likelihood times prior as the unnormalized score for each hypothesis
the normalizing constant P(E) as the sum over all hypotheses
reading the posterior as a belief update
lesson.destination: The learner holds the formula P(H|E) = P(E|H) times P(H) divided by P(E), understands that P(E) = sum over all hypotheses of P(E|H_i) times P(H_i), and can explain why the posterior is proportional to likelihood times prior with the denominator serving only to make probabilities sum to one.

narration.fullText: Imagine a patient tests positive for a rare disease. The test is 99% accurate. Should the patient panic? Bayes' rule -- a formula that tells us how to update a belief when we receive new evidence -- says probably not. Let me show you why. We start with two ideas: the prior probability P(H), which is our initial belief that hypothesis H is true before seeing any evidence, and P(E), the total probability that evidence E occurs at all. Together they give us the joint probability P(H and E) -- the chance that both H is true and E is observed -- computed as P(H) times P(E given H). The term P(E given H), read as the probability of evidence E given that H is true, is called the likelihood -- it measures how well hypothesis H explains the evidence. Now here is the key symmetry: the joint probability P(H and E) can be written two ways. First: P(H) times P(E|H). Second: P(E) times P(H|E), where P(H|E) is the posterior -- our updated belief about H after seeing E. Because both expressions equal the same joint probability, we can set them equal: P(H) times P(E|H) equals P(E) times P(H|E). Dividing both sides by P(E) gives us Bayes' rule: P(H|E) equals P(E|H) times P(H), all divided by P(E). The numerator, likelihood times prior, is the unnormalized score for hypothesis H. The denominator P(E) -- the total probability of the evidence, computed by summing P(E|H_i) times P(H_i) over every possible hypothesis -- rescales everything so the posterior probabilities add to one. That is why the posterior is proportional to likelihood times prior: the denominator is just a normalizing constant. Let us return to the medical example. Suppose the disease affects 1 in 1000 people, so the prior P(disease) equals 0.001. The test has a 99% true-positive rate, so the likelihood P(positive|disease) equals 0.99. The test also has a 1% false-positive rate, so P(positive|no disease) equals 0.01. The normalizing constant P(positive) equals 0.99 times 0.001 plus 0.01 times 0.999, which is about 0.01098. The posterior P(disease|positive) is 0.99 times 0.001 divided by 0.01098, which is roughly 0.09 -- only about 9%. The rare prior crushed the likelihood. Bayes' rule made that precise.

beats.text: Imagine a patient tests positive for a rare disease. The test is 99% accurate. Should the patient panic? Bayes' rule -- a formula that tells us how to update a belief when we receive new evidence -- says probably not. Let me show you why.
beats.whyThisBeat: hook and payoff: name the surprising gap between test accuracy and true probability

beats.text: We start with two ideas: the prior probability P(H), which is our initial belief that hypothesis H is true before seeing any evidence, and the likelihood P(E given H) -- the probability of observing evidence E if hypothesis H is true, measuring how well H explains E.
beats.whyThisBeat: joint probability and likelihood defined -- rung 1 and part of rung 2

beats.text: Together they give us the joint probability P(H and E) -- the chance that both H is true and E is observed. We compute it as P(H) times P(E given H). But we can also write this joint probability the other way: as P(E) times P(H given E). P(H given E) is the posterior -- our updated belief about H after seeing E.
beats.whyThisBeat: conditional probability defined and the symmetry introduced -- rung 2 and rung 3

beats.text: Because both expressions equal the same joint, we set them equal: P(H) times P(E|H) equals P(E) times P(H|E). Dividing both sides by P(E) gives Bayes' rule: P(H|E) equals P(E|H) times P(H), all divided by P(E).
beats.whyThisBeat: Bayes' formula derived from the symmetry -- rung 4

beats.text: The numerator, likelihood times prior, is the unnormalized score for hypothesis H. The denominator P(E) -- computed by summing P(E|H_i) times P(H_i) over every possible hypothesis -- is a normalizing constant that makes all posteriors sum to one. That is why the posterior is proportional to likelihood times prior.
beats.whyThisBeat: likelihood times prior and normalizing constant explained -- rungs 5 and 6

beats.text: Now back to the medical test. The disease affects 1 in 1000 people: prior P(disease) equals 0.001. The true-positive rate is 99%: P(positive|disease) equals 0.99. The false-positive rate is 1%: P(positive|no disease) equals 0.01.
beats.whyThisBeat: concrete numbers plugged into the prior and likelihood slots

beats.text: The normalizing constant P(positive) equals 0.99 times 0.001 plus 0.01 times 0.999, which is about 0.01098. The posterior P(disease|positive) is 0.99 times 0.001 divided by 0.01098 -- roughly 9%. The rare prior crushed the high likelihood. Bayes' rule made that precise.
beats.whyThisBeat: posterior computed numerically and the lesson of the rare-prior effect delivered -- rung 7

beats.text: So: posterior equals likelihood times prior, divided by the normalizing constant. The denominator does not change which hypothesis scores highest -- it only rescales. Update your beliefs with the evidence; let the prior keep you honest about rarity.
beats.whyThisBeat: consolidation and take-away -- posterior interpretation locked in

actions.content: Bayes' Rule: Updating Beliefs with Evidence

A probability tree with a single root node labeled 'Population' on the left. Two branches emerge: upper branch labeled 'H: disease, P(H)=0.001' and lower branch labeled 'not H: no disease, P(not H)=0.999'. From the upper branch, two sub-branches: one labeled 'E: test positive, P(E|H)=0.99' leading to a leaf, and one labeled 'test negative, 0.01' leading to a leaf. From the lower branch, two sub-branches: one labeled 'E: test positive, P(E|not H)=0.01' leading to a leaf, and one labeled 'test negative, 0.99' leading to a leaf. Leaves for 'test positive' paths are highlighted with small filled circles.
Prior P(H) = initial belief before evidence
Likelihood P(E|H) = how well H explains E
P(H and E) = P(H) * P(E|H) = P(E) * P(H|E)
Posterior P(H|E) = updated belief after evidence

Two overlapping circles on a white background. Left circle labeled 'H' (hypothesis true). Right circle labeled 'E' (evidence observed). The overlapping region is shaded and labeled 'H and E'. Below the left circle: P(H). Below the right circle: P(E). Inside the overlap: P(H and E).
box the entire equation P(H and E) = P(H)*P(E|H) = P(E)*P(H|E) in blue to show the symmetry
P(H|E) = [ P(E|H) * P(H) ] / P(E)
box the numerator P(E|H)*P(H) in red to mark it as the unnormalized score
P(E) = sum over all H_i of P(E|H_i) * P(H_i)
underline 'sum over all H_i' in green to emphasize that P(E) pools every hypothesis

A horizontal probability bar spanning the full width. The bar is divided into two segments: a very narrow left segment (width proportional to 0.001) colored dark blue and labeled 'P(H)=0.001 disease' above it, and a wide right segment colored light gray labeled 'P(not H)=0.999 no disease'. Below each segment a smaller sub-bar shows the positive-test fraction: under the dark-blue segment a thin red bar labeled 'TP: 0.99 x 0.001', and under the gray segment a slightly wider red bar labeled 'FP: 0.01 x 0.999'. The two red sub-bars together represent P(E).
all
P(disease) = 0.001
P(pos|disease) = 0.99    P(pos|no disease) = 0.01
circle the result 0.090 in red to emphasize the surprising low posterior despite high test accuracy
underline 0.001 in orange to show the rare prior driving the low posterior
box the denominator P(E) in green to reinforce that it is only a rescaling constant, not changing the ranking of hypotheses
Posterior proportional to Likelihood x Prior. Denominator rescales only.
underline the phrase 'Likelihood x Prior' in red to anchor the proportionality as the core insight
